basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Tīn — “The Fig” — opens with four oaths on places. Qushayrī reads them with contained emotion: these places, he says, are where prophets set down their feet. The fig — the mountain of Damascus, refuge of Jesus; the olive — the hill of Jerusalem, station of all the prophets; Sinai — where Moses became God's interlocutor; the safe land — Mecca, where Abraham raised the House and Muḥammad received the Book. Four oaths, four traces. “Wherever the feet of the beloved (aḥbāb, أَحْبَاب) have rested,” Qushayrī says, “there is a sanctity.” The ground keeps the memory of the saints' prayer. Then comes the heart of the sūra: man created in the most beautiful stature (aḥsani taqwīm, أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيم) — and man cast down to the lowest of the low, except those who believe and do good. The whole teaching gathers there: nobility is not a static gain, it is a fidelity that renews itself. And the sūra closes with a question each must answer within: Is God not the most just of judges?

versets.trad: By the fig and the olive!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī pauses on the two trees with a gardener's attention. He praises the fig first: “God swears by it for the immense favour He has shown the creature: He put no stone in it, He cleansed it of all bitterness, and He made it the size of a mouthful so that the pleasure of eating it might be whole.” The fig is the fruit of sheer generosity — given without effort, without bother, without aftertaste. Then the olive: God placed in it many benefits, including the oil that lights the lamp and the oil that perfumes the bread. Two trees for two faces of the gift: immediate sweetness, and lasting light. But Qushayrī lets the other reading sound too: these same two trees would be the place where Jesus stood — the mountain of Damascus — and the hill where all the prophets paused. At the foot of the creature as at the foot of the prophet, God sets His mark.

mots.def: tīn: the fig tree, or the fig; a blessed tree in the Quranic tradition.
zaytūn: the olive tree, or olives; central tree of Mediterranean culture and a Quranic symbol of light (24:35).

versets.commentaire: Ṭūr Sīnīn (طُور سِينِين): Mount Sinai, where God spoke to Moses. Qushayrī comments in a single but beautiful sentence: “Wherever the foot of the beloved (aḥbāb, أَحْبَاب) has rested, there is a sanctity.” Sinai is not only the scene of a past event; through that passage it has become a different place. The ground keeps the saint's imprint. For the Sufis it is also the image of an inward state: a heart that has been visited once never quite becomes an ordinary heart again.

mots.def: ṭūr: the mountain; construct genitive: “the Mount of”.
sīnīn: proper noun; Mount Sinai where God spoke to Moses. Some commentators hear in it an Ethiopic word meaning “beautiful, blessed”.

versets.commentaire: al-balad al-amīn (البَلَد الأَمِين): “the safe city” — Mecca. Qushayrī goes straight to the heart: “This city has an immense nobility, for it is the city of the Beloved; it shelters the House; and the House of the Beloved, like the city of the Beloved, has its own worth and rank.” Four oaths, then, and the same note sustained: what makes these places holy is not their own substance — it is that they have welcomed the friends of God. The verse now opens upon the only subject that matters: man himself.

mots.def: balad: country, region, city; here Mecca.
amīn: safe, peaceful; same root as amn (security). Names Mecca as inviolable sanctuary.

versets.ar: لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ
versets.tr: la-qad khalaqnā l-insāna fī aḥsani taqwīm
versets.trad: Truly We created man in the most beautiful stature,
versets.commentaire: A pivotal verse. aḥsan taqwīm (أَحْسَن تَقْوِيم): the most beautiful stature, the most upright conformation. The outward sign — that man stands upright, where every other creature has its face stooped to the ground — does not exhaust the meaning. Qushayrī draws from it a very fine theological inference: precisely because God says here that He created man in the most beautiful stature, one must understand that God Himself is beyond stature and beyond form. If creature and Creator shared the form, then the most beautiful in form would be God; but this is said of man. Therefore God is beyond form. And man receives, in his dignity, the reflection of attributes that are — in fullness — the Lord's alone: life, knowledge, will, speech, hearing, sight. The greatness of man is thus a received greatness; and this is why the angels were ordered to prostrate before him (2:34). “Man is a small world, the wise say: all that the cosmos is composed of is gathered within him.”

mots.def: affirmative particle + qad: marks the perfect with emphasis.
khalaqa: to create; perfect 1st pers. plur.
aḥsan: superlative of ḥasan, beautiful; in construct genitive: “the most beautiful of”.
taqwīm: verbal noun of qawwama (to form, to set upright, to shape); just, harmonious conformation.

versets.trad: then We cast him to the lowest of the low,
versets.commentaire: asfal sāfilīn (أَسْفَل سَافِلِين): the lowest of the low. Qushayrī offers two readings that complete one another. The first, natural: it is old age, the age when the mind declines (the Quran's “most abject of life”, 16:70). The second, inward: it is the state of wretchedness (shaqāwa, شَقَاوَة) and denial, where man has let himself fall from the noble stature he had been given. The first fall, man undergoes; the second, he consents to. It is against this second that the sūra warns — and the exception in verse 6 names the only way out: faith and upright deed.

mots.def: radda: to return, to send back, to cast down; perfect 1st pers. plur. + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 1st pers. plur.

mots.def: asfal: the lowest; in construct accusative.
sāfil: low, abject; plural sāfilīn: the lowly.

versets.ar: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ فَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ
versets.tr: illā lladhīna āmanū wa-ʿamilū ṣ-ṣāliḥāti fa-lahum ajrun ghayru mamnūn
versets.trad: save those who believe and perform righteous deeds; for them shall be a reward unceasing.
versets.commentaire: ajr ghayr mamnūn (أَجْر غَيْر مَمْنُون): an “unceasing” reward. Mamnūn, from the root m-n-n, can mean “lessened” or “constantly recalled” (a gift whose giver keeps mentioning it, which spoils it). A reward ghayr mamnūn is therefore at once without diminution and without humiliating reminder. The verse names the one path that saves from abasement: faith, good deeds. Some commentators add that, in this very life, if one holds to practice, one continues to receive the reward of habitual deeds even when sickness or travel keeps one from accomplishing them.

mots.def: mamnūn: passive participle; what is diminished, or what is constantly recalled (making the gift burdensome).

versets.trad: What then will make you deny the Judgment?
versets.commentaire: The verse, by its form, can address the denier (“what makes you deny the Judgment, having seen this?”) or the Prophet as support (“who would treat you as a liar, after these signs?”). The word dīn (دِين) means here the Judgment, but also Religion: to deny one is to deny the other. The verse marvels: after the creation of man from a drop, raised to the “most beautiful stature”, how could anyone still deny the Resurrection?

mots.sens: makes you deny, accuses you of lying
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to deny, to give the lie to; + 2nd pers. masc. sing. suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: dīn: religion or judgment; here, more likely the Judgment.

versets.trad: Is not God the most just of judges?
versets.commentaire: aḥkam al-ḥākimīn (أَحْكَم الحَاكِمِين): the most just, the wisest of judges. The root ḥ-k-m means both “to judge” and “to be wise” — the two senses converge in God. The verse closes the sūra with a rhetorical question that calls for inward assent. According to the ḥadīth already cited, one is to answer balā, “yes indeed”: a man noble in his stature or low in his fall — it is according to what he has done. And God, who distinguishes the two, is the most just of judges. A simple logic, but one that closes the sūra on the responsibility of each soul.

mots.def: defective verb laysa: not to be; in the perfect, present sense.
aḥkam: superlative of ḥakīm; the most just, the wisest. Root ḥ-k-m: to judge, to be wise.
